narrative: As a Black male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.20—right around the typical 3.21 average for students in this dataset—your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on past students' profiles to predict their chances of passing the bar exam on the first try. The model assessed your overall profile, including your Year 1 and Year 3 school decile rankings, LSAT score, family income bracket, and plans to attend full-time, and it calculated a 51% probability that you would fail the bar exam. This tipped the scales just over the threshold, leading to the rejection of your application, as the school prioritizes admitting those predicted to pass with high confidence. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 decile ranking of 1, which is notably lower than the average of 5.53, signaling to the model weaker performance in later undergraduate years compared to peers who succeeded on the bar. On a more positive note, your undergraduate GPA had a strong counterbalancing effect, pulling the prediction toward success because it aligns so closely with what successful bar takers typically show. Your middle family income bracket also helped nudge things in a favorable direction, as it matches a level associated with slightly better outcomes than lower brackets in the dataset. While your LSAT score of 14.50 is well below the 36.70 average and your Year 1 decile of 2 is lower than the typical 5.71, these played lesser roles here compared to the top three factors. Full-time enrollment, like most applicants at 92.7%, didn't sway things much either way. In your specific situation, the model saw your dip in Year 3 performance as the biggest red flag relative to typical students who pass, outweighing your GPA and background strengths despite your full-time commitment. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a Black man pursuing law—many with similar profiles have succeeded by addressing key gaps—but the prediction reflects patterns from past data. To move forward, focus on boosting your LSAT through targeted prep, as it's a common lever for applicants in your position, and consider retaking undergrad-level courses or gaining legal experience to strengthen later academic showings. Reapplying after these improvements could shift your predicted odds significantly, opening doors at schools that value resilience like yours. Keep pushing; your GPA shows you have the foundation to build on.
